title: Kid's Adirondack Chair
summary: A scaled-down Adirondack chair sized for a child, with the same slatted seat, fan-back, and wide flat arms as the full-size original.
description:
This is a straightforward scale-down of the classic Adirondack chair, sized so a child's feet reach the ground and the low, reclined seat angle still works at the smaller size. The seat and back slats fan out at the same relative angles as a full-size chair, just shorter, which is what keeps the proportions looking right rather than like a shrunken adult chair.

Build the two side frames first — each one leg-to-arm assembly cut and glued as a mirrored pair — since the seat slats and back slats both depend on the side frames being identical for the chair to sit square and not rock. Attach the seat slats to the side frames next, then the back slats, following the same fan-angle layout as the seat.

The wide flat arms are the signature Adirondack detail and are perfectly serviceable as a place to rest a drink or a coloring book. **Round over all edges and corners with a router or sanding block** — this chair is sized for a child, and their instinct to climb on and off it (and each other) means all edges should be genuinely blunt, not just eased.

tools.note: Curved arm and back slat top cuts.
Rounding edges; a sanding block works as an alternative.

cutList.note: Top ends cut with a gentle curve.

steps.title: Build the side frames
steps.body: Cut the front and back legs and join into two mirrored side frame assemblies. Confirm both sides match before continuing.

steps.title: Attach the seat
steps.body: Attach the seat support cleats to the inside of the side frames, then screw the seat slats across the top with even gaps.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Cut the gentle curve into the tops of the back slats with a jigsaw. Attach the back support brace between the side frames, then fan and attach the back slats across it, following the same angle layout as the seat.

steps.title: Attach the arms and finish
steps.body: Attach the arms to the tops of the side frames and back slats. Round over all edges and corners with a router or sanding block, since this chair is sized for a child. Sand and apply an exterior sealer or paint.

unresolvedImages.alt: Small Adirondack chair sized for a child
